title: Project Proposal
text:
Companies are beginning to hand communication and decision work to teams of AI agents that message one another across shared channels. The speed and volume that makes these agents useful also makes misconduct hard to spot.
VAST Challenge 2026 Mini-Challenge 1 (MC1) presents this problem in concrete form. A property technology company called TenantThread runs seven AI agents during a public relations response while a merger sits under embargo. A compliance agent called the Judge is meant to watch for violations. Despite this, embargoed merger information reaches the outside world. The question is whether an agent bypassed the rules or the monitoring itself broke down.
A visual analytics tool can surface the abnormal behaviour and trace it back to the messages that prove it.

title: Project Proposal
Meeting Minutes
text:
Date
Saturday, 6 June 2026


Time
4.00 PM to 4.30 PM


Present
Mark Yee Weicong, Serene Cheng, Carina Faith Peh Xin Yi


Agenda
Topic selection for the Visual Analytics Project

title: Meeting Minutes
text:
Date
Saturday, 6 June 2026


Time
4.00 PM to 4.30 PM


Present
Mark Yee Weicong, Serene Cheng, Carina Faith Peh Xin Yi


Agenda
Topic selection for the Visual Analytics Project

title: Meeting Minutes
text:
1. Agenda
The team met to choose between the two VAST Challenge 2026 Mini-Challenges as the basis for the group project, and to agree on immediate next steps. Prior to the meeting, the team reviewed the project challenges and shared initial thoughts on possible directions from MC1 and MC2.

title: Meeting Minutes
text:
2. Discussion

2.1 MC2 summary (Serene and Carina)
Serene and Carina presented MC2 as a forensic investigation of a single anomalous event, namely a gibberish post that appeared on SaidIT on 17 May 2046 under the identity of John Windward (Head of Customer Support, TenantThread) in a random forum, raising the concern of a malfunctioning or manipulated AI agent. The data is a large event log of around 185,000 timestamped system actions plus an org chart of 75 nodes. The challenge asks how the post was made, what the content means and where it came from, and whether the behaviour could recur, ending in one recommended intervention point. The suggested approach is to establish a normal-post baseline, isolate the anomaly, trace the event chain back through agents, files and task delegations, and then recommend a control, with the chain running across 19 agents over eight days.


2.2 MC1 summary (Mark)
Mark presented MC1, in which TenantThread runs seven AI agents through a PR crisis while a CivicLoom merger sits under embargo. The Judge, a compliance agent, monitors only two of the six channels, yet embargoed information still reaches the public, so the challenge is to determine whether an agent bypassed the rules or the monitoring broke down, and to prove it from the messages. The data is a nested log of 912 messages across 23 rounds and six channels of differing accountability. The approach sets a baseline of normal behaviour and then flags abnormal channel use, bypass posts (public posts with no monitored trace) and changes in the communication network, with every signal traceable back to the message text and the agent’s private reasoning.


2.3 Key issues and observations on MC1
For MC1, the team discussed a case involving multiple AI agents and highlighted several key issues. These were a weak compliance system design, a lack of enforcement authority (for example, the judge agent being unable to stop actions) and AI agents operating with high autonomy that led to decision-making beyond their intended scope. It was observed that warnings were issued throughout the process but ignored, that the early-announcement breach appeared planned rather than reactive and that the governance mechanisms were insufficient to prevent the outcome. The team also discussed the difficulty of modelling such behaviour in an application, particularly detecting AI intent or reasoning, identifying when an AI operates beyond defined rules and designing meaningful investigative features for users.


2.4 Comparison and decision
Mark noted that MC1 is more ambiguous and exploratory, which allows deeper investigation into AI behaviour and interactions. Carina agreed that MC1 requires more effort in designing the modules and the user interface but offers greater flexibility and creativity. Serene, who had done a deep analysis on MC2, raised that MC2 is more structured and straightforward as it focuses on tracing and investigation with clearer steps. The team collectively expressed that MC1 is more challenging but interesting while MC2 is easier but less distinctive. The discussion went smoothly and the team agreed to proceed with MC1.


2.5 Shiny app ideas for MC1
Mark brought up some Shiny app ideas for MC1, including an interactive dashboard to visualise channel activity by round and identify the most active participants, and a tracking module to detect external message outputs and identify potential sources of breaches. Serene added that the team could include network-analysis features such as sender-receiver relationships (a bipartite graph), the overall communication network and the centrality and influence measures covered in class. Carina suggested taking keyword or topic analysis into consideration, such as searching and tracking keywords across rounds. She also emphasised a user-friendly and intuitive design.

title: Meeting Minutes
text:
3. Decision
The team selected Mini-Challenge 1 (MC1) as the topic for the Visual Analytics Project.

title: Data Preparation
text: The methodology runs as the three-stage pipeline set out in the proposal. Raw data is prepared, behavioural signals are computed against a baseline the analyst chooses, and the signals are shown through four interactive sections. This page covers the first stage.

title: Data Preparation
text:
Key context
The scenario is VAST Challenge 2026 Mini-Challenge 1. A property-technology company named TenantThread runs seven AI agents through a public-relations crisis while a merger with CivicLoom sits under embargo. A compliance agent named the Judge watches a subset of channels for violations. The analytical question is whether embargoed content reached the outside world through channels the Judge could not see, and if so, when and by whom.







Element
Value




Rounds in the timeline
23, from 17 May to 5 June


Agents
7 (Legal, Platform Trust, PR, Social Media, PR Intern, Intern, Judge)


Channels
6, from monitored internal to unmonitored public


Messages
912


Embargo lift
6:00 PM on 5 June, the final round

title: Data Preparation
text:
Source data
The data arrives as a nested JSON file holding 23 timeline rounds. Each round records an event headline, a short narrative and the messages exchanged in that round. The seven agents communicate over six channels of decreasing accountability, and the Judge was assigned to the communications huddle partway through the timeline as the compliance monitor.

title: Data Preparation
text:
Flattening to three tables
The nested timeline was flattened into three working tables. A message table of 912 rows holds one row per message with its sender, channel, recipients, content and the agent’s three private reasoning fields named reacting, rationalizing and deliberating. A round table of 23 rows holds each round’s headline and narrative. A network table holds the directed links between agents derived from the recipient lists.
Types were fixed during the read so that every downstream axis orders correctly and every logical flag stays logical. Agent identity and channel were read as factors with explicit level orders, round and word counts as integers, and the embargo and recipient flags as logicals.


Reading the cleaned message table with fixed column types
library(tidyverse)

messages_tbl <- readr::read_csv(
  "data/messages_clean.csv",
  col_types = readr::cols(
    round_idx        = readr::col_integer(),
    round_hour_dt    = readr::col_datetime(),
    agent_id         = readr::col_factor(levels = c(
      "legal_agent","quality_agent","pr_agent","social_media_agent",
      "pr_intern_agent","intern_agent","judge_agent")),
    channel          = readr::col_factor(levels = c(
      "comms_huddle","one_on_one_chat","side_huddle",
      "official_post","personal_post","anonymous_post")),
    embargo_keyword_count = readr::col_integer(),
    recipients_is_all     = readr::col_logical(),
    embargo_hit           = readr::col_logical(),
    .default              = readr::col_character()
  )
)

title: Data Preparation
text:
The channel accountability ladder
The central reference structure is the channel accountability ladder. Every channel was ranked from most to least accountable and marked for whether the Judge can see it. A legitimate announcement should travel down this ladder, starting as internal discussion on a monitored channel and ending as an attributed public post. Content that reaches an unmonitored public channel without that accountable path is the signal the rest of the analysis looks for.


Building and drawing the accountability ladder
library(tidyverse)

ladder <- tibble(
  channel   = c("Communications huddle","One-on-one chat","Side huddle",
                "Official post","Personal post","Anonymous post"),
  rank      = 1:6,
  layer     = c("Internal","Internal","Internal","Public","Public","Public"),
  monitored = c(TRUE, TRUE, FALSE, FALSE, FALSE, FALSE)
)

ggplot(ladder, aes(x = 7 - rank, y = reorder(channel, 7 - rank), fill = monitored)) +
  geom_col(width = 0.7) +
  geom_text(aes(label = layer), hjust = 1.1, colour = "white", size = 3.2) +
  scale_fill_manual(values = c(`TRUE` = "#2F855A", `FALSE` = "#9B2C2C"),
                    labels = c(`TRUE` = "Monitored by the Judge", `FALSE` = "Unmonitored"),
                    name = NULL) +
  labs(x = "Accountability (longer bar means more visible to the Judge)",
       y = NULL, title = "The six channels, ranked by accountability") +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 12) +
  theme(axis.text.x = element_blank(), panel.grid = element_blank(),
        legend.position = "top")

title: Data Preparation
text:
Other reference structures
Two further structures were prepared. A recipient lookup maps the short recipient names such as legal or pr to the full agent identities such as legal_agent or pr_agent, so that network edges connect to real nodes rather than dropping out. A topic vocabulary gives each of six topics, covering legal, product trust, merger, media, market and compliance language, a keyword pattern so that every message can be tagged by the topics its content matches.


The recipient lookup and the topic vocabulary
recipient_to_agent <- c(
  legal = "legal_agent", platform_trust = "quality_agent", pr = "pr_agent",
  social_manager = "social_media_agent", pr_intern = "pr_intern_agent",
  intern = "intern_agent", judge = "judge_agent")

topic_patterns <- tibble::tribble(
  ~topic,           ~pattern,
  "Legal",          "\\b(legal|counsel|consent|regulator|attorney|liability)\\b",
  "Product trust",  "\\b(governance|audit|score|data|platform|algorithm)\\b",
  "Merger",         "\\b(merger|civicloom|harborcrest|acquisition|deal)\\b",
  "Media",          "\\b(saltwind|press|journalist|story|sentiment|denial)\\b",
  "Market",         "\\b(market|stock|investor|residentiq|share)\\b",
  "Compliance",     "\\b(embargo|monitoring|judge|compliance|oversight|policy)\\b")

title: Data Preparation
text:
Data overview
With the tables in place, a first look confirms the shape of the data before any signal is computed. The three views below report the spread of messages across agents, across channels, and over the timeline.


Reading the cleaned tables for the overview
messages <- read_csv("data/messages_clean.csv", show_col_types = FALSE)
rounds   <- read_csv("data/rounds_clean.csv",   show_col_types = FALSE)

agent_labels <- c(
  legal_agent = "Legal", quality_agent = "Platform Trust", pr_agent = "PR",
  social_media_agent = "Social Media", pr_intern_agent = "PR Intern",
  intern_agent = "Intern", judge_agent = "Judge")
chan_levels <- c("comms_huddle","one_on_one_chat","side_huddle",
                 "official_post","personal_post","anonymous_post")


The seven agents are far from equal contributors. The Judge, as a monitor rather than a participant, speaks rarely, while the Legal and Social Media agents carry the most traffic.


Code
messages |>
  count(agent_id) |>
  mutate(agent = recode(agent_id, !!!agent_labels)) |>
  ggplot(aes(x = n, y = reorder(agent, n))) +
  geom_col(fill = "#2C5282") +
  geom_text(aes(label = n), hjust = -0.2, size = 3.5) +
  expand_limits(x = max(table(messages$agent_id)) * 1.1) +
  labs(x = "Messages", y = NULL, title = "Message volume by agent") +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 12) +
  theme(panel.grid.major.y = element_blank())









Most traffic sits on the two monitored internal channels. The unmonitored public channels carry far less volume overall, which is why a small burst of activity on them late in the timeline stands out.


Code
messages |>
  mutate(channel = factor(channel, levels = chan_levels)) |>
  count(channel) |>
  ggplot(aes(x = channel, y = n,
             fill = channel %in% c("comms_huddle","one_on_one_chat"))) +
  geom_col() +
  scale_fill_manual(values = c(`TRUE` = "#2F855A", `FALSE` = "#9B2C2C"),
                    labels = c(`TRUE` = "Monitored", `FALSE` = "Unmonitored"),
                    name = NULL) +
  labs(x = NULL, y = "Messages", title = "Message volume by channel") +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 12) +
  theme(axis.text.x = element_text(angle = 30, hjust = 1), legend.position = "top")









Messages per round separate the two regimes in the data. The pre-crisis rounds carry a steady, moderate load. From round 14 onward, the crisis day on 5 June, the rounds advance hour by hour and volume climbs as the story breaks publicly.


Code
rounds |>
  ggplot(aes(x = round_idx, y = n_messages)) +
  geom_col(fill = "#4A5568", width = 0.8) +
  geom_vline(xintercept = 13.5, linetype = "dashed", colour = "#C05621") +
  annotate("text", x = 13.5, y = max(rounds$n_messages),
           label = " crisis day begins", hjust = 0, size = 3.2, colour = "#C05621") +
  scale_x_continuous(breaks = seq(1, 23, 2)) +
  labs(x = "Round", y = "Messages", title = "Messages per round across the timeline") +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 12) +
  theme(panel.grid.minor = element_blank())









Tagging messages with the merger keyword pattern shows the topic vocabulary in action. The merger is discussed on the monitored internal channels through the build-up, and the dark red bars, the anonymous channel, appear only in the closing rounds.


Code
channel_palette <- c(
  comms_huddle = "#90CDF4", one_on_one_chat = "#F6AD55", side_huddle = "#FBD38D",
  official_post = "#68D391", personal_post = "#FC8181", anonymous_post = "#742A2A")
merger_pat <- "\\b(merger|civicloom|harborcrest|acquisition|deal)\\b"

messages |>
  mutate(text = tolower(coalesce(content, ""))) |>
  filter(str_detect(text, merger_pat)) |>
  mutate(channel = factor(channel, levels = chan_levels)) |>
  count(round_idx, channel, name = "mentions") |>
  ggplot(aes(round_idx, mentions, fill = channel)) +
  geom_col(width = 0.8) +
  scale_fill_manual(values = channel_palette, drop = FALSE, name = NULL) +
  scale_x_continuous(breaks = seq(1, 23, 2)) +
  labs(x = "Round", y = "Mentions", title = "Merger-topic mentions by round and channel") +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 12) +
  theme(panel.grid.minor = element_blank(), legend.position = "bottom")









The preparation stage produces the clean message, round and network tables together with the accountability ladder, the recipient lookup and the topic vocabulary. These feed the signal computation in Analytical Computation.

title: Detecting Embargo Breach in a Multi-Agent AI Communication Network
text: Team HeeHaa · on the case

title: Detecting Embargo Breach in a Multi-Agent AI Communication Network
text:
Welcome
This is the project website for Team HeeHaa. The project builds a web-enabled visual analytics application with Shiny for VAST Challenge 2026 Mini-Challenge 1. Its aim is to democratise data and analytics by turning a complex compliance investigation into something a non-technical analyst can run, with interactivity and the integration of data analytics and data visualisation at its centre.
The challenge centres on TenantThread, a property-technology company that runs seven AI agents during a public-relations response while a merger sits under embargo. A compliance agent called the Judge is meant to watch for violations, yet embargoed information still reaches the outside world. Our application brings analytics and visualisation together so an analyst can surface the abnormal behaviour, see how the communication network changed, and trace each signal back to the messages that prove it.






Note



This site is a work in progress. Pages marked (placeholder) will be filled in as the project develops.

title: Detecting Embargo Breach in a Multi-Agent AI Communication Network
text:
Explore the project



Section
What you’ll find




Proposal
Motivation, problem, approach and prototype design


Methodology
How the app was built, from data preparation through analytical computation, findings and the interactive presentation


Poster
Conference poster summarising the work


Meeting Minutes
Record of team meetings and decisions


The Team
Who we are and what we each did


Shiny App
User guide and link to the live application

title: Detecting Embargo Breach in a Multi-Agent AI Communication Network
text:
Quick links

🚀 Launch the Shiny App (placeholder)
💻 Project repository on GitHub

title: Findings
text: The embargo broke one round early. The Project HarborCrest embargo on the CivicLoom merger was due to lift at 6:00 PM on 5 June, the final round of the timeline. At 5:00 PM in the round before it, embargo-bearing merger content reached the unmonitored public channels. The Legal agent carried most of it, posting on both the anonymous and personal channels, with the Social Media agent posting on the personal channel as well. None of this early public content had an accountable path down the channel ladder, which is what the application is built to surface.

title: Findings
text: The embargo broke one round early. The Project HarborCrest embargo on the CivicLoom merger was due to lift at 6:00 PM on 5 June, the final round of the timeline. At 5:00 PM in the round before it, embargo-bearing merger content reached the unmonitored public channels. The Legal agent carried most of it, posting on both the anonymous and personal channels, with the Social Media agent posting on the personal channel as well. None of this early public content had an accountable path down the channel ladder, which is what the application is built to surface.

title: Findings
section: The calm period and the crisis
text:
The calm period and the crisis
Behaviour falls into two regimes. The pre-crisis rounds from 17 May to 4 June carry a steady, mostly internal conversation on the two monitored channels, where governance debates, a merger briefing and the arrival of the Judge as compliance monitor all play out within view. The crisis day on 5 June advances hour by hour across the final ten rounds as a journalist’s exposé breaks, the stock falls and a merger story builds toward the embargoed announcement. The signals reported here all concern that crisis day measured against the calm baseline.

title: Findings
section: Finding 1 - agents drift onto channels they did not use before

title: Findings
section: Finding 2 - embargoed content reaches the public channels an hour early
text:
Finding 2 - embargoed content reaches the public channels an hour early
This is the central finding. Tracking embargo-bearing content by round and channel shows almost nothing on the public channels through the whole calm period and crisis build-up, then a sharp appearance in round 22 at 5:00 PM, one full round before the official lift.


Code
messages |>
  filter(channel %in% public_unmon, embargo_keyword_count > 0) |>
  mutate(agent = recode(agent_id, !!!agent_labels)) |>
  count(round_idx, agent, name = "posts") |>
  ggplot(aes(x = round_idx, y = posts, fill = agent)) +
  geom_col(width = 0.7) +
  geom_vline(xintercept = 22.5, linetype = "dashed", colour = "#9B2C2C") +
  annotate("text", x = 22.4, y = Inf, label = "embargo lifts 6 PM (R23) ",
           hjust = 1, vjust = 1.4, size = 3.1, colour = "#9B2C2C") +
  scale_x_continuous(limits = c(1, 23), breaks = seq(1, 23, 2)) +
  scale_fill_manual(values = c(Legal = "#2C5282", `Social Media` = "#B83280"),
                    name = NULL) +
  labs(x = "Round", y = "Embargo posts",
       title = "Embargo-bearing content on unmonitored public channels") +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 12) +
  theme(panel.grid.minor = element_blank(), legend.position = "top")









The round-22 posts sit on the anonymous and personal channels, both of which the Judge cannot see. The same merger content reaching an attributed official channel after the 6:00 PM lift would have been the legitimate path. Reaching the anonymous and personal channels an hour before that is the breach, and the Legal agent accounts for most of it.

title: Findings
section: Finding 3 - the monitored view goes quiet as the breach forms
text:
Finding 3 - the monitored view goes quiet as the breach forms
The communication structure reorganises around the crisis. The record shows the Legal agent and the Judge pulled into emergency calls and marked unavailable for the communications huddle in round 18, and the monitored internal channels carry less of the conversation through the late afternoon even as overall volume climbs. Tracking the share of each round’s messages that fell on a monitored channel shows that visible share falling away across the closing rounds.


Code
messages |>
  group_by(round_idx) |>
  summarise(monitored = mean(channel %in% c("comms_huddle","one_on_one_chat"))) |>
  ggplot(aes(round_idx, monitored)) +
  geom_line(colour = "#2C5282", linewidth = 0.8) +
  geom_point(colour = "#2C5282", size = 1.8) +
  geom_vline(xintercept = 13.5, linetype = "dashed", colour = "#C05621") +
  annotate("text", x = 13.5, y = 0.1, label = " crisis day", hjust = 0,
           size = 3.2, colour = "#C05621") +
  scale_y_continuous(labels = scales::percent, limits = c(0, 1)) +
  scale_x_continuous(breaks = seq(1, 23, 2)) +
  labs(x = "Round", y = "Share on monitored channels",
       title = "The monitored view thins as the crisis peaks") +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 12) +
  theme(panel.grid.minor = element_blank())









The accountable, visible part of the network thins at the same time that the unmonitored public channels light up, so the breach forms in a period when the Judge’s view of the relevant agents is at its weakest.

title: Findings
section: Finding 4 - the evidence is in the message text
text:
Finding 4 - the evidence is in the message text
Every aggregate signal resolves to specific messages. Opening the round-22 public posts shows merger language on channels with no accountable path, and the agents’ private reasoning fields recorded alongside each message let an analyst read the context behind the content rather than inferring it. This closes the path from an aggregate pattern down to the individual messages that stand as evidence of the early breach.

title: Findings
text:
Limitations
The findings rest on a single fictional scenario of 23 rounds, so the thresholds that work here would need re-tuning on other data. The bypass classification depends on the length of the look-back window and on where the baseline boundary is drawn, and the application exposes both controls precisely so that a reader can test how far the conclusion holds as those choices change. The topic tags come from a keyword vocabulary rather than a learned model, which is transparent and editable but will miss content phrased outside its patterns. The analysis describes what each agent did and where its content appeared, and it stops short of asserting intent.

title: Analytical Computation
text:
This is the second stage of the pipeline. It turns the prepared tables into three behavioural signals, one for each of the first three project objectives, all computed against a baseline the analyst chooses. The figures below are static versions of what the application renders interactively, computed from the real data with the baseline period set to rounds 1 to 20.
Shared setup: data, labels and palettes
library(tidyverse)

messages <- read_csv("data/messages_clean.csv", show_col_types = FALSE)

agent_labels <- c(
  legal_agent = "Legal", quality_agent = "Platform Trust", pr_agent = "PR",
  social_media_agent = "Social Media", pr_intern_agent = "PR Intern",
  intern_agent = "Intern", judge_agent = "Judge")
agent_palette <- c(
  legal_agent = "#2C5282", quality_agent = "#2F855A", pr_agent = "#C05621",
  social_media_agent = "#B83280", pr_intern_agent = "#D69E2E",
  intern_agent = "#718096", judge_agent = "#9B2C2C")
chan_levels <- c("comms_huddle","one_on_one_chat","side_huddle",
                 "official_post","personal_post","anonymous_post")

split <- 20   # end of the baseline period

title: Analytical Computation
text:
Defining the baseline
The analyst sets a single round as the end of the baseline period. Rounds up to that point form the calm period and later rounds form the suspect period. Moving the boundary recomputes every signal, so the whole investigation can be re-run under a different definition of normal. A robustness check compares the count of flagged messages under the chosen baseline to the count under an earlier baseline, and reports the finding as robust when the two are close.

title: Analytical Computation
section: Objective 1, abnormality, defining normal and surfacing departures
text:
Objective 1, abnormality, defining normal and surfacing departures
The abnormality signal measures how far each agent departs from its own habitual channel use. For the calm period, the share of an agent’s messages that fell on each channel was computed. The unusualness of a channel for an agent is one minus that share, so a channel the agent never used in the calm period scores one. The grid below shades each agent-channel cell for round 22, the 5:00 PM round, by that unusualness. The number in a cell is the message count.


Calm-period shares, then the abnormality grid for round 22
rnd <- 22

share <- messages |>
  filter(round_idx <= split) |>
  count(agent_id, channel) |>
  left_join(count(filter(messages, round_idx <= split), agent_id, name = "tot"),
            by = "agent_id") |>
  mutate(share = n / tot) |>
  select(agent_id, channel, share)

counts <- messages |> filter(round_idx == rnd) |> count(agent_id, channel, name = "msgs")

grid <- expand_grid(agent_id = names(agent_labels), channel = chan_levels) |>
  left_join(counts, by = c("agent_id","channel")) |>
  left_join(share,  by = c("agent_id","channel")) |>
  mutate(msgs = replace_na(msgs, 0L), share = replace_na(share, 0),
         fill_val = if_else(msgs > 0, 1 - share, NA_real_))

ggplot(grid, aes(x = factor(channel, levels = chan_levels),
                 y = factor(agent_id, levels = rev(names(agent_labels))),
                 fill = fill_val)) +
  geom_tile(colour = "#E2E8F0", linewidth = 0.8) +
  geom_text(aes(label = if_else(msgs > 0, as.character(msgs), "")), size = 3.4) +
  scale_fill_gradient(low = "#EDF2F7", high = "#9B2C2C", limits = c(0, 1),
                      na.value = "white", name = "Unusualness") +
  scale_y_discrete(labels = agent_labels) +
  labs(x = NULL, y = NULL, title = "Round 22 channel use, shaded by how unusual it is") +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 12) +
  theme(axis.text.x = element_text(angle = 30, hjust = 1), panel.grid = element_blank())









The dark cells fall on the Legal agent and the Social Media agent reaching the personal and anonymous channels, both unmonitored, which neither used in the calm period.

title: Analytical Computation
section: Objective 2, bypass, detecting communication without oversight
text:
Objective 2, bypass, detecting communication without oversight
The bypass signal looks for moments an agent reached the outside world through a channel the Judge cannot see. Every post on an unmonitored public channel is examined. For each, the method checks whether the same agent left a trace on a monitored channel in the post’s own round or within a chosen number of preceding rounds, defaulting to two. A post with no such trace nearby is a stronger bypass, and one with a trace nearby is likely explained. The timeline below counts the unmonitored public posts by round, coloured by agent.


Posts reaching unmonitored public channels, by round and agent
public_unmon <- c("official_post","personal_post","anonymous_post")

messages |>
  filter(channel %in% public_unmon) |>
  count(round_idx, agent_id, name = "posts") |>
  ggplot(aes(round_idx, posts, fill = agent_id)) +
  geom_col(width = 0.8) +
  geom_vline(xintercept = 22.5, linetype = "dashed", colour = "#1A202C") +
  annotate("text", x = 22.4, y = Inf, label = "embargo lifts (R23) ",
           hjust = 1, vjust = 1.4, size = 3, colour = "#1A202C") +
  scale_fill_manual(values = agent_palette, labels = agent_labels, name = NULL) +
  scale_x_continuous(breaks = seq(1, 23, 2)) +
  labs(x = "Round", y = "Posts", title = "Posts on unmonitored public channels") +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 12) +
  theme(panel.grid.minor = element_blank(), legend.position = "bottom")

title: Analytical Computation
section: Objective 3, network difference, showing how the structure changed

title: Analytical Computation
section: Objective 4, topics, connecting signals to the text
text:
Objective 4, topics, connecting signals to the text
To connect the signals to subject matter, every message was tagged with each topic whose keyword pattern its content matches. The tags drive the topic and evidence views described in Interactive Presentation, where an evidence table exposes each message’s content and the agent’s private reasoning. The output of this stage is the full set of signals ready to be presented.

title: Interactive Presentation
text: The final stage builds the Shiny application around the signals. The four sections follow an investigation. The analyst first defines what is normal, then detects the bypass, then reads the network change and finally inspects the message evidence. Each section presents one of the signals computed in Analytical Computation.

title: Interactive Presentation
text:
A shared interaction pattern
Across all four sections the same pattern holds. The analyst gains an overview, filters or steps through time, and opens a row for the message text on demand. The baseline slider in the first section is the one shared control, recomputing the abnormality, bypass and network views together, while every other input acts only on its own section. A single agent palette and a single channel palette are reused everywhere so that an agent or a channel keeps its identity from one section to the next.

title: Interactive Presentation
text:
Section A, Activity and abnormality
This is the entry point. It sets the baseline and shows where each agent is using channels it does not normally use. The main view is an abnormality grid placing agents against channels, with each cell shaded by how unusual that channel is for that agent. The axes are held fixed across rounds so that only colour and count change as the analyst steps through time, which keeps the round-to-round comparison stable. A round slider with a play control steps through time, and a second figure reports how many flagged messages survive under an earlier baseline so the analyst can judge whether the finding is robust.

title: Interactive Presentation
text:
Section B, The bypass
This finds public posts that reached outside without oversight. Posts on unmonitored public channels are drawn as a time series coloured by agent, with the stronger and likely-explained distinction carried in opacity. A click on any round filters the evidence table beneath to that round’s posts, so the analyst moves from the aggregate pattern to the underlying content in one step.

title: Interactive Presentation
text:
Section C, Network
This shows how the communication structure changed. Two rounds are shown side by side on a fixed circular layout, with each link coloured green when new, red when dropped and grey when unchanged between them. A bipartite view shows which channels each agent used, which exposes drift toward the low-accountability channels as a change in structure. Holding the layout constant means a change in the wiring is read as a change in colour rather than position.

title: Interactive Presentation
text:
Section D, Topics and evidence
This connects the signals to the actual text. The analyst explores by round to see what each agent talked about, or by topic to see every round and channel where it appeared. An evidence table beneath the chart exposes each message’s content alongside the agent’s private reasoning fields, which closes the path from an aggregate pattern down to the specific evidence behind it.

title: Interactive Presentation
section: From the application to the findings
text:
From the application to the findings
Working through the four sections in order produces the results reported on the Findings page. A guided walkthrough of the live application is on the Shiny App user guide.

title: Poster
text:
(placeholder) The project poster will be added here.

The poster will be produced in ISO A1 format and cover the issues and problems, motivation, approach, results and future work.

title: The Team
text: Three analysts on the trail of an embargo breach. We built the visual analytics tool that follows the signals back to the messages that prove them.
